segments.s162-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. یک پاس بگذشت is an intransitive past event: یک پاس ('one watch/turn') is the period/interval and بگذشت is the past-tense verb 'passed' (third-person singular). از introduces the source/setting 'from/during', and تیره شب is a poetic adjective+noun order meaning 'dark night'.
segments.s162-g2: چنان چون is a redundantly emphatic simile 'just as / as if'. کسی is an indefinite subject 'someone'. راز گوید uses the present literary form گوید (literary present/epic-present used for vividness) with راز as its object 'speaks/tells a secret'. به تب is an adverbial phrase 'in fever' where به marks the manner/condition.
segments.s162-g3: خروشی is a nominal subject 'a roar / a tumult'. برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose / issued forth' (third-person singular). ز is the poetic variant of از meaning 'from', marking the source, and افراسیاب is the proper-name source.
segments.s162-g4: بلرزید is past tense 'trembled' (third-person singular). بر جای is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on the place of...', where بر is the preposition. آرام و خواب are coordinated nouns modifying جای ('the place of calm and of sleep'); together they form the phrase that undergoes the trembling (a metonymic way to say rest/sleep was disturbed).
segments.s162-g5: پرستندگان is the plural subject 'the worshippers / devotees'. تیز here functions adverbially 'swiftly / quickly'. برخاستند is the past plural verb 'they rose / sprang up' (third-person plural).
segments.s162-g6: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'to roar' used here as part of the action they perform; و links the coordinated elements; غلغل is a noun meaning 'uproar / clamor'; آراستند is the past plural verb 'they adorned/arrayed/arranged' used transitively — together the clause means they set up or filled the scene with a roar and uproar.
segments.s162-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (introducing a clause). آمد is the past tense 'came / reached'. به + proper name (به گرسیوز) means 'to Garsivaz' (direction/recipient). آن آگهی is 'that news/information' (آن demonstrative + آگهی 'information/news').
segments.s162-g8: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. شد is the past of شدن 'became' indicating a change of state. تیره is an adjective 'dark/ominous'. دیهیم is an adjective meaning 'rural/peasant-like'. شاهنشهی (شاهنشاهی) refers to 'the imperial rule/sovereignty' and is the noun whose state is being described: 'the imperial rule became dark and rural.'
segments.s162-g25: This is a rhetorical interrogative phrase: کجا (kajā) 'where' opens a question meaning 'where (ever) have I seen...'. چون here means 'like/as' and modifies شب تیره 'a dark night'. The verb دیده‌ام is perfect (past) 'I have seen' and comes at the end (typical Persian SOV order: object/nominal phrase before the verb).
segments.s162-g26: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. پیر و جوان is the idiomatic pair 'old and young'. نیز 'also/too' emphasizes inclusiveness. نشنیده‌ام is the negative perfect 'I have not heard (of/learned)' (ن- negation prefixed to شنیده‌ام). The clause answers/continues the first by saying the speaker hasn’t even heard of such a night from anyone.
segments.s162-g27: This is an example of Persian object-before-verb order: بیابان پر از مار 'a wilderness/full of snakes' is placed before the verb دیدم 'I saw'. به خواب is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in (a) dream' (به + خواب). پر از uses پر 'full (of) + از' to mark what it is full of.
segments.s162-g28: This line is elliptical/parallel: جهان پر ز گرد 'the world full of dust' and آسمان پر عقاب 'the sky full of eagles'. ز after پر is the poetic 'of/from'. گرد here means 'dust' (not the verbal sense). The repeated construction (noun + پر + ز/Ø + complement) creates parallel images.
segments.s162-g29: زمین خشک شخی is a noun with descriptive adjectives: زمین 'earth' modified by خشک 'dry' and شخی (an adjectival/participial form meaning roughly 'cracked/sundered'). که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; گفتی here is intended as 'you said', but the provided sense list does not include the verbal sense (see senses mapping). سپهر is a poetic word for 'the sky/heavens' that the speaker apparently attributes in the relative clause.
segments.s162-g30: بدو is the prepositional or dative-like 'to it/unto it'. تا + بود gives a temporal clause 'as long as/while there was (the) world' (تا = 'until/while', بود = past 'was'). ننمود is the negative past/punctual verb 'did not show' (ن- negation prefixed to نمود), and چهر 'face' is its object: 'the sky did not show its face to it while the world existed.'
segments.s162-g31: سراپرده 'inner curtain/tent' is the head noun, من 'my' is the possessor, and زده is a participial/past adjective 'struck/put' (here meaning 'pitched' or 'set up'). بر کران is a locative phrase 'on the edge/brink' with بر 'on/upon' followed by کران 'edge'. The verb is implicit in the participial usage.
segments.s162-g32: به گردش is an adverbial phrase 'in a circuit / moving round' (به + گردش). سپاهی is a collective noun 'a troop/army'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. کندآوران appears to be a group-name or agent noun here; the supplied senses do not match a clear lexical meaning in this context, so it is left as an unspecified proper/agentive term.
segments.s162-g9: به تیزی is an adverbial phrase where به + تیزی ('swiftness') functions adverbially as 'swiftly'. بیامد is the simple past (3rd person) 'he came'. به نزدیک شاه is a directional phrase 'toward/near the king' with به + نزدیک (adverb/adjective) + noun; word order places the manner, verb, then the goal.
segments.s162-g10: ورا is an old pronominal clitic-like adverb meaning 'toward him' or 'him' (i.e., 'he saw him'). دید is past 'saw'. بر خاک خفته is a prepositional/locative phrase: بر + خاک ('on the ground') with خفته (participle/adjective) 'lying/asleep' modifying خاک. به راه closes the phrase indicating location 'in/along the road'; the order places the verb before the locative phrase.
segments.s162-g11: به بر در گرفتش is a compound spatial/action phrase: به + بر + در (to/at the door) + گرفتش (past 'took/seized' + enclitic pronoun -ش 'him'). بپرسید is past tense 3rd singular 'he asked'. زوی is an archaic prepositional/pronominal form meaning 'from him' or 'of him' — here marking the person questioned ('he asked of him').
segments.s162-g12: که introduces the content of the question (a subordinate/interrogative clause). این داستان is the direct object 'this story/tale'. با here functions as a preposition 'to/with' in the sense of addressing the brother (classical usage can mean 'to' or 'for' someone). برادر is the addressee 'brother'. بگوی in this context is an archaic subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'say/tell' (the line asks who told the tale to the brother); the form is terse/poetic.
segments.s162-g13: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'thus gave answer' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer') — i.e., 'he replied thus'. که introduces the following command/clause. پرسش مکن is an imperative negative: پرسش (question) + مکن (negative imperative 'do not [do]'), so 'do not ask'.
segments.s162-g14: مگو is the negative imperative 'do not say / speak'. این زمان is an adverbial phrase 'this time / now'. ایچ is an emphatic negative 'at all / nothing' (negating the speech). با من سخن is the complement 'speak with me' (با + من + سخن). The word order is imperative + time/emphasis + indirect object phrase: 'Do not at this time at all speak with me.'
segments.s162-g15: بمان is the imperative 'stay/remain' (2nd person). تا introduces a purpose/temporal clause 'until'. خرد بازیابم is a verb phrase where بازیابم is 1st person singular present/subjunctive 'I regain/restore' and خرد 'intellect/wits' is its object ('until I regain my wits'). یکی here means 'one' or 'someone' (a person to be taken), acting as an indefinite object for the following verb.
segments.s162-g16: به بر گیر is an imperative sequence: به + بر + گیر literally 'take up / seize upon' (گیر is imperative 'take/catch'). و links the two commands. سختم بدار is an imperative 'hold/keep my severity' or more idiomatically 'keep him under restraint/hold him firmly' — بدار here is an imperative 'keep/hold' and سختم (سخت + م) expresses 'my sternness/harshness' or 'hold him tightly' in context. اندکی is an adverb 'a little / for a short while' modifying the command.
segments.s162-g113: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'); غمی شد is a verbal phrase where غمی (a noun 'grief') + شد (past of شدن 'to become/occur') means 'grief arose/seized (him)'. بشنید is a past tense verb 'he heard' placed after چو to complete the 'when he heard' clause; the proper name افراسیاب is the subject of بشنید.
segments.s162-g114: نکرد is the past negated verb 'did not do'; ایچ is an emphatic negative 'at all/not at all' modifying the verb. بر جنگ جستن is an infinitival phrase 'to seek/enter battle' (بر = 'to/against', جنگ = 'battle', جستن = verbal infinitive 'to seek/go after'). شتاب here is a noun 'haste' (used adverbially); the whole order means 'he did not, at all, make haste to go to battle.'
segments.s162-g115: به marks the indirect object ('to/for'); گرسیوز (Garsivaz) is the recipient. آن رازها ('those secrets') is the direct object; برگشاد is a past form meaning 'opened' or 'revealed' (poetic past participle/verb), so the phrase means 'he revealed those secrets to Garsivaz.'
segments.s162-g116: نهفته سخنها is a noun phrase with the adjective نهفته 'hidden' before سخنها 'sayings/words' ('many hidden sayings'). بسی is an adverbial/quantifying word ('much'/'many') and کرد یاد is a light-verb construction where یاد (remembrance) takes کرد ('did/made') to mean 'he recalled' or 'he brought to mind.'
segments.s162-g117: که‌گر is an older/poetic conditional 'if'. من is the subject 'I'. به جنگ سیاوش سپاه places the goal and opponent before سپاه: به جنگ سیاوش = 'to/against Siavash in battle' and سپاه = 'the host/army' (object of the verb that follows). The sequence sets up the conditional 'if I (do not) lead the army to fight Siavash.'
segments.s162-g118: نرانم is the 1st-person singular negative present/past form of راندن 'to drive/lead' meaning 'I do not lead' (here 'I will not lead/if I don't lead'). نیاید is a negative present/future-like form 'will not come/does not come'. کسی in this negative clause functions as 'no one'. کینه‌خواه is a compound 'kineh-خواه' meaning 'one who desires hatred/vengeance' ('desiring vengeance'), with خواه used in its sense 'to want/desire.'
segments.s162-g119: نه ... و نه sets up a paired negation 'neither ... nor'. او کشته آید combines کشته (past participle 'killed/slain') with آید (present/optative of آمدن) to form a passive/future-like sense 'he will be killed' or 'he shall be slain'—here negated by نه: 'he will not be slain in battle.' به جنگ marks the place/means 'in battle.'
segments.s162-g120: برآساید is a verb meaning 'to be eased/appeased' or 'to find relief' (here in the negative correlative 'nor will I find relief'). از introduces the source 'from' and گفت و گوی انجمن is a genitive-like phrase 'the talk/speech of the assembly' (گفت و گو 'conversation/talk', انجمن 'assembly/council'). The clause therefore means 'nor will I be relieved from the council's talk.'
segments.s162-g81: ازان پس (a fixed temporal phrase) = "after that/thereupon"; بگفت is the past narrative verb "he said/related" (بـ- prefix + گفت). آنچ در خواب دید is a relative clause "that which he saw in sleep/dream" (آنچ = آن‌چـه "that which"; در خواب = "in a dream"; دید = past "saw"). The clause order is typical classical Persian: temporal adverbial → verb of speaking → direct content (what was said).
segments.s162-g82: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. موبد (mōbed) is the agent. ز شاه = 'from the king' (poetic preposition ز = از). آن سخنها شنید = 'heard those words' (شنید past). The whole clause gives the time/circumstance when the preceding report was made.
segments.s162-g83: بترسید is a past-tense/perfect stem meaning 'he became afraid' or 'he feared.' و = 'and.' ز شاه = 'from/of the king.' زنهار خواست is an idiomatic compound: زنهار (a noun 'warning/forbearance' or entreaty) + خواست ('asked/requested' in past) → 'he begged/implored (for) forbearance/mercy from the king.'
segments.s162-g84: که introduces an indirect question/clause: 'that/how...' این خواب را = definite object 'this dream' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). کی here = 'who'/'how' in rhetorical sense ('who can...'); توان is the modal auxiliary 'can/able to'; گفت here is the verb 'to say' (classical past/infinitive sense within the indirect question); راست = 'true'. The clause asks rhetorically whether the dream can be declared true.
segments.s162-g85: مگر here means 'unless' (introducing an exception/condition). شاه با بنده پیمان کند = 'the king should make a covenant with the servant'; کند is the present/subjunctive or optative form used in classical narrative to express possibility/wish ('may he make' or 'should he make'). Word order: conditional particle → subject (شاه) → prepositional phrase (با بنده) → object (پیمان) → verb (کند).
segments.s162-g86: زبان را = 'the tongue' as definite object (را). به پاسخ = 'as/for an answer' (به + noun). گروگان کند = 'make (it) a hostage' (گروگان = 'hostage/pledge'; کند again = 'make'). The infinitival/nominal sequence states the consequence/terms of the covenant: hold the tongue as pledge for a reply.
segments.s162-g87: کزین = 'from this/therefrom' (indicates basis). در سخن = 'in speech/what is said'; هرچ = 'whatever' (indefinite relative); داریم = present plural 'we have/possess' (here 'we recall' or 'we have in mind'); یاد = 'remembrance' / 'what is remembered.' The phrase collects whatever recollections they have to present.
segments.s162-g88: گشاییم is a jussive/subjunctive 1st-person-plural form 'let us open/reveal/present' (from گشودن). بر شاه = 'before/to the king' (بر + شاه). و = 'and'. یابیم is subjunctive/optative 'may we obtain/find' (یافتن); داد here means 'justice' (دادِ حق) rather than the past tense 'gave.' The clause expresses the intended action and hoped-for result: present our recollections to the king and obtain justice.
segments.s162-g217: جز از ... is an exception phrase: 'except for' or 'apart from'. تخت زرین is a noun (تخت, 'throne') followed by the adjective زرین ('golden') in normal Persian order. The relative clause introduced by که ('that/which/where') attaches to تخت زرین: او شاه نیست is a simple present-tense negation ('he is not king') with نیست as the copular negative. Here که functions as a relative connector meaning 'of which/where', so the whole segment reads 'except for the golden throne, of which he is not king'.
segments.s162-g218: تن پهلوان uses تن ('body' but idiomatically 'the person/self') before پهلوان ('champion, hero') to indicate the hero himself. از + درگاه ('from the court') uses از to mark separation or non-belonging; درگاه is a compound meaning 'royal court/presence' (در literally 'gate/door' + گاه forming the place 'threshold/court'). The clause ends with نیست ('is not'), so 'تن پهلوان از درگاه نیست' means 'the champion does not belong to / has no place at the court.'
segments.s162-g121: نه at the start marks a negative clause; کاووس is the subject and خواهد is the present/future auxiliary meaning “will/wishes” (poetic, without می). ز من = “from/against me” (poetic preposition). نیز here = “also/too,” and کین is an archaic noun meaning “vengeance” or “grudge.” The clause literally reads “Kavus will not will vengeance from me also,” i.e. “Kavus will not seek revenge against me.”
segments.s162-g122: The second negative clause mirrors the first with نه introducing a parallel negation. آشوب (turmoil) is the subject, گیرد is the 3rd-person present/future of گرفتن (poetic form without می) meaning “seize/take,” and سراسر زمین is a prepositional phrase “throughout the land.” Together: “nor will turmoil seize the whole land.”
segments.s162-g123: بجای + noun + infinitive expresses seeking worldly things: بجای جهان جستن = “to seek the world” (i.e., worldly gain). جستن is an infinitive (“to seek”) that is coordinated with کارزار by the conjunction و, so both ‘seeking the world’ and ‘(seeking) battle/engaging in battle’ are the actions being rejected.
segments.s162-g124: مبادم is an archaic optative/negative wish meaning “may it not be (that) I…” and governs the earlier infinitives; به جز = “except,” آشتی = “reconciliation/truce,” and هیچ کار = “no deed/affair.” The whole clause expresses a wish not to be engaged in anything but peace: “may I do no action except reconciliation.”
segments.s162-g125: فرستم is 1st-person present/future “I will send.” به نزدیک او is a prepositional phrase “to/near him” (i.e., to his presence or court). سیم و زر are coordinated direct objects “silver and gold.” The simple word order (verb + prepositional phrase + objects) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s162-g126: همان is a demonstrative “that very/the same,” followed by a list of items connected by و: تاج و تخت (crown and throne) and فراوان گهر (abundant jewels). فراوان modifies گهر (an adjective/adverbial intensifier placed before the noun in poetry).
segments.s162-g127: مگر here means “unless” (introducing an exception). کاین is an archaic fused demonstrative “these/that these,” and بلاها is the plural “calamities.” ز من بگذرد uses بگذرد (3rd-person present/subjunctive of گذشتن, poetic without می) meaning “pass (away) from me / be removed from me.”
segments.s162-g128: که introduces the content of the fear (“that …”). ترسم is 1st-person singular present “I fear.” روانم = “my soul/spirit” (روان + possessive -م). فرو is an adverb “down/utterly/away” placed before پژمرد (3rd-person present/future of پژمردن, poetic) giving the sense “to wither completely.” The clause reads “for I fear that my soul will wither away.”
segments.s162-g105: سراسر پر آشوب گردد زمین is a poetic word order: the adverb 'سراسر' (entire/throughout) + adjectival phrase 'پر آشوب' (full of turmoil) precede the verb 'گردد' (from گشت/گرد; 'become') and the subject 'زمین' comes last. 'گردد' here is the classical present/subjunctive form used to express becoming / a future-like result ('will become' or 'shall be').
segments.s162-g106: ز بهر سیاوش بجنگ و به کین: 'ز بهر' is a fixed poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of / because of'; 'سیاوش' is the personal name. 'بجنگ' is the infinitive/substantive use of the verb 'to fight' (بـ + جنگ), here indicating 'for war / for fighting'. The conjunction 'و' links the parallel prepositional phrase 'به کین' ('به' + noun) meaning 'for vengeance'.
segments.s162-g107: بدانگاه is a temporal adverb 'at that moment'. یاد آیدت is a verbal phrase where یاد ('remembrance') is the noun and آیدت is the verb 'comes to you' (آید + enclitic ت for 'to you'); together they mean 'the remembrance/you will remember' or 'it will come to your mind'. راستی (truth) is the direct object / theme being recalled.
segments.s162-g108: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. ویران شود is a passive/experiential construction where ویران (ruined) + شود (become) yields 'will be ruined' (شود used in its present/subjunctive sense with future implication). کشور is the subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion). از کاستی uses از + noun to mean 'because of / from lack/deficiency'.
segments.s162-g109: جهاندار‌گر is the conditional/imaginary 'اگر' fused to 'جهاندار' ('world-lord' / ruler of the world) meaning 'if the world-ruler'. مرغ گردد literally 'becomes a bird' where گردد is the present/subjunctive of 'become'. بپر (ب + پر) is the subjunctive/imperative-form of 'پر زدن' 'to fly' and here follows the conditional clause — in classical Persian the short form expresses the result 'would fly' or 'should fly'.
segments.s162-g110: برین is the demonstrative/adverbial 'بر + ین' meaning 'to that / up there'; چرخ گردان is a compound noun 'the revolving wheel/heaven' (چرخ = sky/heaven, گردان = revolving). نیابد گذر is negative + verb + noun: نیابد (ن + یابد) 'does not/ would not find' and گذر 'a passage' — together 'would find no passage'. The negative is attached to the verb, common in classical style.
segments.s162-g111: برین سان is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner' (بر + ین + سان). گذر کرد خواهد سپهر arranges the verb complex in a poetic order: گذر (passage) + کرد (make/do) + خواهد (will) + سپهر (heaven). The normal modern order would be 'سپهر خواهد گذر کرد'; here the auxiliaries are split and the subject 'سپهر' comes last — overall meaning 'the heaven will make the passage / thus will the heaven pass'.
segments.s162-g112: گهی ... و گهی ... is a classical correlative 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. پر ز is the 'full of' construction (پر = full (of) + ز = of/from), so 'پر ز خشم' = 'full of anger' and 'پر ز مهر' = 'full of affection/love'. The repeated structure contrasts alternating states of the heavens.
segments.s162-g57: خروشیدمی is an archaic/past 1st‑person form meaning “I cried out” (classical Persian past). The phrase places the adverbial فراوان after the pronoun من and before the source phrase ز درد, so literally “I cried out plentifully/from pain.” ز introduces the cause (‘from/because of’).
segments.s162-g58: م + را here together mark the object مرا (“me”); the dataset splits them but they form the object of کرد. ناله و درد = “moaning and pain” joined by و. بیدار کرد uses کرد in the causative sense “caused (to be) awake,” so (مرا) is the person affected: “(my) moaning and pain caused (me) to wake.”
segments.s162-g59: بدو here means “to him / unto him” (dative) and precedes گفت and the speaker’s name in an inverted order common in poetry: “to him said Garsivaz.” The printed lemma for گفت in the data is not the usual verbal sense; syntactically گفت is the past tense verb “said.” این خواب شاه is vocative/identifying: “this dream, O king.”
segments.s162-g60: نباشد is a classical negative present/subjunctive form used here to assert ‘is not’ or ‘can be no…’; جز از = “except/from” (جز introduces exclusion, از gives source). کامه نیک‌خواه = “the desire/wish of a well‑wisher.” The clause says the dream is nothing except what comes from a benevolent wish.
segments.s162-g61: This clause is an optative wish introduced by باید/باشد-type subjunctive: 'همه کام دل باشد' = 'may every desire of the heart be (so/fulfilled)'. Word order is standard Persian: determiner 'همه' + noun phrase 'کام دل' (literally 'desire of the heart'), followed by the verb 'باشد' in the subjunctive/optative sense. The conjunction 'و' links this wish to the following noun phrase 'تاج و تخت' ('crown and throne').
segments.s162-g62: 'نگون گشته' is a compound of adjective/participle + participle-verb meaning 'has become overturned/overthrown' (passive/resultative). The preposition 'بر' governs the following noun phrase and here means 'upon/over', while 'بدسگال تو بخت' groups as 'your ill‑fated fortune' with the adjective 'بدسگال' preceding the possessor 'تو' and the head noun 'بخت'. Together the clause expresses the wish that crown and throne be brought low upon (or fall to) your ill‑starred luck.
segments.s162-g63: This is a nominal predication: 'گزارنده خواب' ('the reporter/interpreter of the dream') is the subject, and 'باید کسی' uses the modal 'باید' ('should/must') with the predicate 'کسی' ('someone'). In Persian the modal 'باید' follows the subject and precedes the indefinite predicate; the clause means 'the dream‑interpreter ought to be someone...'.
segments.s162-g64: The relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/that') modifies 'کسی'. The structure 'از دانش اندازه دارد بسی' literally 'has a measure/degree of knowledge enough' uses 'از' + 'دانش' ('of knowledge'), 'اندازه' as 'degree/extent', and the verb 'دارد' ('has'). The final adverb 'بسی' ('sufficiently/much') intensifies the amount; overall: 'who has a considerable degree of knowledge.'
segments.s162-g33: This clause is a compact past action: 'یکی باد برخاستی' = 'a certain/a gust of wind rose.' 'یکی' is an indefinite determiner ('a certain'); 'باد' is the noun 'wind.' 'برخاستی' is an archaic past form meaning 'rose' or 'arose.' The phrase 'پر ز گرد' follows the noun and uses the adjective 'پر' with the preposition-like particle 'ز' to mean 'full of,' so word-order is N (wind) + verb (rose) + modifier (full of dust).
segments.s162-g34: 'درفش مرا سر نگونسار کرد' is a caused-state construction: X را Y کرد = 'made X Y.' 'درفش' is the subject/affected item 'banner/standard.' 'م' here is the left part of the object clitic 'مرا' (rendered separately in the segmentation) but its listed senses do not match the pronominal role, so it is marked separately below. 'را' is the definite/object marker attaching to 'درفش'+'م' to show the direct object. 'سر' is the body-part noun 'head' and 'نگونسار' is an adjectival/participial description 'downcast/mournful'; 'کرد' is the causative verb 'made/caused.' The whole means 'caused my banner's head to be downcast.'
segments.s162-g35: This clause describes emergent motion or outflow: 'برفتی' here functions as a past verb meaning 'poured forth/ran' (an archaic poetic form); the given lexical senses did not include this exact meaning, so sense is set to -1. 'ز هر سو' is a prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every side'). 'یکی جوی خون' is the subject/phrase 'a stream of blood' where 'یکی' = 'a certain' and 'جوی' = 'stream.'
segments.s162-g36: 'سراپرده و خیمه گشتی نگون' describes the affected places and their resulting state. 'سراپرده' = the inner curtained chamber/pavilion, 'و' = and, 'خیمه' = tent. 'گشتی' here is a poetic/archaic verbal/adjectival element indicating change of state ('became'/'were made'); the provided senses ('patrolling/roving') do not capture that function so its sense is set to -1. 'نگون' = 'overturned/overthrown' describes their condition.
segments.s162-g37: 'وزان لشکر من فزون از هزار' opens with the connective 'وزان' = 'and from that' (linking back to the action). 'لشکر من' = 'my army' (possessive structure; 'من' serves as the possessor). 'فزون از هزار' = 'more than a thousand' with 'فزون' indicating an excess and 'از' introducing the comparison 'than a thousand.'
segments.s162-g38: This is a terse description of casualties: 'بریده سران' = 'with heads cut off' where 'بریده' is a past-participial/adjectival form 'cut off' modifying 'سران' (heads). 'و تن افگنده' = 'and bodies cast down' with 'تن' = 'body' and 'افگنده' = 'cast/thrown down.' 'خوار' is a final adjective meaning 'despised/humbled.' The clause lacks an explicit finite verb because Persian poetic style often strings participial descriptions after numerals.
segments.s162-g39: 'سپاهی ز ایران چو باد دمان' is a simile: 'سپاهی ز ایران' = 'soldiers from Iran.' 'چو' introduces the simile 'like/as.' 'باد دمان' is a poetic image literally 'the wind's hem/skirt' (دمان = skirt/hem), so the line likens the soldiers' movement or spread to the trailing rush of the wind.
segments.s162-g40: 'چه نیزه به دست و چه تیر و کمان' enumerates armaments with an exclamatory/indefinite 'چه' meaning 'whether/whatever' (i.e., 'some with... some with...'). 'نیزه به دست' = 'spear in hand' where 'به' functions adverbially ('by/with' = 'in (their) hand') and 'دست' = 'hand.' The second 'چه' parallels the first, and 'تیر و کمان' = 'arrow and bow.' The construction lists alternatives or variety among the soldiers.
segments.s162-g17: زمانی is a temporal adverb 'at one time / once'; برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/stood up'. چو here is the conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; آمد is the simple past 'came'; به هوش is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'into awareness' (به + هوش) together meaning 'came to his senses'. The clause is slightly telegraphic (verb before subject), the subject follows in the next segment.
segments.s162-g18: جهان‌دیده is an epithet 'the one who has seen the world / well‑traveled, experienced'; با ناله و با خروش is an adverbial manner phrase 'with moaning and with a cry', with the repeated با for rhythmic emphasis. The whole phrase names the subject and describes how he rose.
segments.s162-g19: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed / set' with its object شمع 'candles' following; the coordinating و links to برآمد 'arose', here a third‑person past 'he rose' (the narrative subject switches back to the previously mentioned person). به تخت = 'onto the throne' (به indicating direction/position).
segments.s162-g20: همی is an older/perfective‑progressive adverb meaning 'continually / was still'; بود is the past of 'to be' (here 'was'); لرزان is a predicative adjective 'trembling'. بسان درخت is a simile 'like a tree'. Word order (adverb + copula + adjective) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s162-g21: بپرسید is a past tense verb 'asked' (3rd person); the name and epithet گرسیوز نامجوی follow the verb (verb‑first order is common in classical Persian). نامجوی is an epithet/appositive attached to the name, here 'Namjuy' (literally 'name‑seeker' as a sobriquet).
segments.s162-g22: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause. بگشای is a jussive/imperative form 'open' (literally 'let him open' or an instruction 'open'); لب 'lip' is the object of that verb and in the idiom 'لب گشودن' means 'to speak'. زین is an archaic contraction meaning 'از این' ('of/about this' or 'from this')—the provided lemma senses don't list this sense, so it functions here as 'about this'; شگفتی 'wonder' is the object, and بگوی is the complementary verb 'speak / tell' (here coordinated with بگشای to mean 'open your lips and tell').
segments.s162-g23: چنین گفت is the stock narrative formula 'thus said'; the verb گفت 'said' precedes the speaker's name (پرمایه افراسیاب), where پرمایه is an epithet 'rich/abundant' attached to the name. This is simple past narrative word order.
segments.s162-g24: که introduces the reported speech/statement. هرگز ... نبیند is a classical negative‑indefinite construction: هرگز 'never' + کسی 'anyone' + present subjunctive نبیند ('should see') conveys 'no one has ever seen'. این 'this' is the object. به خواب is a prepositional phrase 'in a dream' (به used as 'in'), so the whole means 'that never has anyone seen this even in a dream.'
segments.s162-g49: مرا (me + object marker را) is the direct object 'me'; پیش کاووس = 'into the presence of Kavus' (preposition + name); بردی is a past-tense verb 'brought/took' (classical/epic past form); دوان is a participial/adverbial form 'running/rushing' that modifies the following nominal phrase (i.e. indicates that someone came running). Word order places the object before the verb (typical Persian), and the adverbial 'دوان' follows the verb here to describe the manner.
segments.s162-g50: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / a' introducing a person; بادسر here functions as a proper name (Badsar); نامور is an adjective 'renowned/famed' modifying پهلوان 'champion/hero'. The noun follows its modifiers in this epic register, giving 'a certain Badsar, a renowned champion.'
segments.s162-g51: یکی here functions as 'there was / a certain' introducing existence; تخت is the subject 'throne'; بودی is the past copula 'was' (epic/past ending -ی); چو = 'as/like' introduces a simile; تابنده ماه = 'shining moon' (تابنده modifying ماه). The clause literally says 'There was a throne, like the shining moon.'
segments.s162-g52: نشسته = 'seated' (predicate/adjectival); برو here is the fused prepositional phrase بر + او 'upon it' (i.e. 'upon that [throne]'); پور = 'son (of)' placed before the father's name as in epic style; کاووس شاه = 'Kavus, the king.' Together: 'seated upon it was the son of Kavus the king.'
segments.s162-g53: دو هفته = 'two weeks' (دو modifies هفته); نبودی is the past negative/absence 'was not / had not been' (epic past form); ورا = dative 'to/for him'; سال بیش = 'more than a year' (سال 'year' + بیش 'more'). The clause gives a duration: 'He had been away two weeks; for him it was over a year.'
segments.s162-g54: چو = 'when'; دیدی is past 'saw' (third-person past form in epic register); مرا = 'me' (م + را, direct object); بسته = 'bound/tied' describing the speaker's condition; در پیش خویش = 'in front of himself / before him' (در 'in', پیش 'presence of', خویش with enclitic ش = 'his own'). The clause means 'When he saw me bound before him...'.
segments.s162-g55: دمیدی is a past verb 'you blew/struck' (classical/epic past); به کردار = 'in the manner/way of' (به + کردار); غرنده = 'roaring' (adjective); میغ = 'blade/sword' used here as the image of the blow. Together the phrase describes the action as 'you struck in the manner of a roaring blade.'
segments.s162-g56: میانم = 'my middle / the middle of me' (میان + possessive -م); بدو = 'to/into it' (به + او) indicating direction/instrument ('into the middle with it'); نیم کردی = 'you made (me) half' = 'you halved / cut (me) in two' (نیم 'half' + کردی 'you did/made'); به تیغ = 'with the sword' (instrument phrase). The whole means 'you cut me in two with the sword.'
segments.s162-g153: شود is a literary/wish-like 3rd-person present (hortative) meaning 'may it become/let it be'. The noun phrase در جهان چشمه آب ('the spring of water in the world') uses poetic word order with the locative در جهان up front and the noun چشمه followed by its modifier آب and the adjective خشک after the noun as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s162-g154: نگیرد is the 3rd-person negative jussive/subjunctive ('may it not ...') and here links back to the previous adjective خشک: خشک نگیرد = 'may it not become dry'. The prepositional phrase به نافه درون ('into/for the inner navel/cavity') precedes the noun phrase بوی مشک ('the scent of musk'), which comes last by poetic order; the verb negates that the musk-scent be taken/cease in the inner part.
segments.s162-g155: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and introduces the source 'کژی' (crookedness). گریزان is a participial adjective 'fleeing/avoiding' modifying the implied subject; شود is the hortative 'may it become' and راستی ('truth/uprightness') is the notion that is being brought into being — the clause has inverted, poetic order.
segments.s162-g156: پدید آید is a two-part verbal idea 'appear / come forth' (پدید 'appear' + آید 'comes'); the following prepositional/locative phrase از هر سوی کاستی means 'from every direction/quarter of lack/deficiency' and indicates the scope from which shortcomings are removed or where truth becomes manifest.
segments.s162-g157: کنون is an adverb 'now'. دانش و داد is a coordinated object phrase 'knowledge and justice' (داد here = 'justice'). یاد آوریم is a 1st-person-plural hortative/imperative 'let us bring to mind / let us recall' (آوریم = 'we bring/let us bring').
segments.s162-g158: بجای is a preposition 'instead of / in place of'. غم و رنج ('sorrow and hardship') are the items to be replaced, and داد آوریم repeats the hortative 'let us bring justice' with داد as the noun 'justice' placed before the verb (typical Persian word order in poetry).
segments.s162-g159: برآساید is a literary intransitive verb 'may (it) find ease / be relieved'. از ما 'from/of us' indicates the source or cause of that relief, زمانی is an adverb 'for a while', and جهان 'the world' is the subject placed at the end by poetic order.
segments.s162-g160: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it should not / must not (be that)'; که introduces the subordinate clause. مرگ آید از ناگهان is 'death comes suddenly' (آید = 'comes/happens', از ناگهان = 'unexpectedly/suddenly'), so the whole clause states that it should not be that death arrive unexpectedly.
segments.s162-g41: همه نیزهاشان is a possessive noun phrase: همه (‘all’) modifies نیزهاشان (here representing نیزه‌ها + شان, “their spears”). سر آورده is a compound verbal expression where سر (literally “head/top”) names the part raised and آورده is a past participle/past form “have brought/raised.” بار follows as a noun meaning “load/burden,” describing the state of the spears. Word order is typical of epic Persian: determiner + possessed noun, then participial predicate and a following noun that qualifies the preceding action.
segments.s162-g42: وزان is a connective/relational particle here linking from the previous noun (‘with/from that’). هر سواری is ‘each/ every rider’ (هر + سواری). سری در کنار is a short prepositional phrase: سری (here ‘head’/‘chief’) combined with در کنار (‘beside/at the side of’). In classical Persian the small preposition در pairs with کنار to mean ‘beside,’ so the phrase places that ‘head’ alongside each rider.
segments.s162-g43: بر تخت من is a locative prepositional phrase (‘upon my throne’), with بر as the preposition and تخت من the object (تخت='throne', من='my' as object/possessive). تاختندی is an archaic past plural form ‘they charged/they galloped.’ The subject سوار (‘riders/horsemen’) follows the verb here — inversion common in poetry — so the clause means ‘riders charged upon my throne.’
segments.s162-g44: This phrase lists the attackers: سیه‌پوش (‘black-clad’) and نیزه‌وران (‘spearmen’). The numeral phrase صد هزار (a hundred thousand) applies to the whole group; in Persian descriptive nouns/adjectives can precede the numeral which then quantifies them. The construction is an apposition enumerating the force.
segments.s162-g45: برانگیختندی is an archaic past plural ‘they roused/they lifted (up).’ ز جای is a prepositional phrase ‘from the place/seat’ (ز = from, جای = place), and نشست here is a noun meaning ‘seat/place of sitting’ rather than a finite verb. Together the clause says ‘they roused [me] from my seat.’
segments.s162-g46: مرا is the object ‘me’ (here split into م + را in the word list); را is the definite/object marker. تاختندی repeats the past plural ‘they charged’ with مرا as its object. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here giving immediacy or continuity), and بسته دست is a participial phrase where بسته (‘bound/tied’) modifies دست (‘hand(s)’), i.e. ‘they bound my hands.’ The overall order places the object before the verb and uses a participle after the adverb to describe the result.
segments.s162-g47: نگه کردمی is a light-noun + verb construction: نگه (‘a look/gaze’) plus کردمی (archaic past ‘I did’) yields ‘I looked.’ نیک modifies the verb phrase as an adverb ‘well/carefully.’ هر سو (‘every direction’) is the locational phrase, and بسی (‘enough/much’) intensifies extent — ‘in every direction very/far.’
segments.s162-g48: ز پیوسته is a prepositional/adverbial phrase (‘continuously/constantly’ — ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from’ used in such adverbial constructions). پیشم (‘before me/in my presence’) is followed by نبودی (‘was not’ — past negative), and کسی (‘anyone/someone’) is the indefinite noun that, with the negative verb, yields ‘there was no one.’ The clause therefore means ‘no one was continually before me.’
segments.s162-g97: بران is an adverbial particle meaning “upon/because of that,” linking this clause to what came before. طالع او را is a genitival/accusative phrase: طالع “fate” modified by او “his,” with را marking the phrase as definite/emphatic. گسی is an indefinite/agentive word here (“a certain one, someone”) placed before the verb; کرد is the past tense verb “did/made/sent.” شاه (the subject) appears at the end in typical poetic inversion: “the king sent someone.”
segments.s162-g98: که introduces a purpose/result clause here (“so that/that”); این بوم is “this land/realm.” گردد is a present/subjunctive-like form of ‘to become’ used here to express the resulting state. بما is to be read as “for us / to us,” and بر تباه is an idiomatic phrase meaning “utterly ruined” (بر intensifies تباه). The whole clause expresses the intended/resulting ruin of the land for the speaker’s side.
segments.s162-g99: اگر begins a conditional clause (“if”). با + personal name (با سیاوش) marks the opponent/partner of the action (“with Siawash”). کند is the present/past-form verb ‘does/makes’ used here to mean ‘wages’ (war). شاه is the subject, and جنگ is the object/nominal complement: literally “if the king makes war with Siawash.” The word order (verb before subject) is a poetic inversion but grammatically standard in Persian poetry.
segments.s162-g100: چو here means “when” (temporal). دیبه is a fine silk/brocade used metaphorically; شود is the copular/auxiliary ‘become.’ روی گیتی is a possessive/genitive-like phrase “the face/surface of the world,” with روی as “surface/face” and گیتی “the world.” به رنگ is an adverbial phrase “in color / coloured,” where به + noun forms an adverbial description. The clause paints the image: when the world’s surface becomes like brocade in color.
segments.s162-g101: ز here is the poetic form of از meaning “from / among.” ترکان is the group “the Turks.” نماند is a present/future-like negative “will not remain / there will not be left.” کسی پارسا is the nominal phrase “a pious person / any pious one”; in the negative construction with نماند it yields the meaning “no pious person will remain.” Word order places the negated verb before the nominal for emphasis (poetic).
segments.s162-g102: غمی گردَد is an idiomatic copular construction: غمی “a grief/sorrow” + گردد “becomes/comes to be,” here meaning that sorrow will arise. از جنگ او marks the cause/source: “from/ because of his war.” پادشا (placed at the end) is the logical subject/topic “the king” — i.e. the king’s war will be the source of sorrow (or the king will be made sorrowful by his war). The line uses poetic word order by placing the predicate before the subject.
segments.s162-g103: وگر is a conditional conjunction meaning “and if / but if.” او is the pronoun “he.” شود کشته is a passive/perfect-like construction: شود (become) + کشته (slain) = “is slain.” بر دست شاه literally “on the king’s hand” idiomatically means “by the king’s hand” (i.e. killed by the king). The verb phrase comes before the subject (شاه appears later as agent), following classical/poetic word order.
segments.s162-g104: به توران sets the locative/dative frame “to/for Turan” (i.e. in Turan). نماند is the negative existential “will remain not / there will be none left.” سر و تاج و گاه is a triad of items: سر “head/chief,” تاج “crown,” and گاه here (= seat/time) meaning the throne/seat — together “neither head nor crown nor seat” (no leaders, no crown, no throne). The coordinated nouns list what will be lost in Turan.
segments.s162-g161: دو بهر = 'two parts/sides' (دو modifies بهر). بهر here is an archaic/poetic 'side; part' and is followed by از جهان 'of the world'. زِیر پای is a prepositional phrase 'under the foot(s)' placed before the copula. منست is an archaic contracted copula phrase meaning 'is mine / belongs to me' (literally 'is of me'), so the clause reads literally 'two sides of the world are under my foot.'
segments.s162-g162: به ایران و توران = 'in/over Iran and Turan' (به here marks the domain/territory). سرای منست: سرای is used in the sense 'house/abode/realm' (poetic noun) and follows the place list, and منست again is the archaic 'is mine / is (my)'. Word order places the place phrase before the predicate.
segments.s162-g163: نگه کن is an imperative sequence: نگه (a noun/verb 'look, behold') + کن (imperative auxiliary 'do'), so 'look!' or 'see!'. که introduces the content clause 'that / how'. چندین ز کندآوران = 'many / several of the کندآوران' where ز is the poetic form of از 'of/from' and کندآوران is a plural agent noun (archaic/poetic; context indicates 'tribute-gatherers' or 'raiders/collectors').
segments.s162-g164: بیارند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form 'they bring' (bi- + ārand). هر سال = 'every year' (هر modifies سال). باژ گران = a noun + adjective: باژ here (poetic/archaic) denotes tribute/booty/noise depending on context; گران is an adjective meaning 'heavy' or 'valuable/precious' placed after the noun (typical adjective order in Persian poetry).
segments.s162-g165: گر = 'if' (conditional particle). ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly 'in this manner / thus' (here marking the manner). باشید is 2nd-person plural subjunctive/present 'you are / you be' used in the protasis (if-clause). همداستان is an adjective/noun meaning 'of one mind, agreed' or 'like-minded' describing the subject 'you'.
segments.s162-g166: به رستم = 'to Rostam' (prepositional phrase marking the recipient). فرستم is 1st-person singular present/future 'I will send / I send' (the poet-speaker's verb). یکی داستان = 'one tale/story' with یکی preceding the noun (a typical poetic word order). The whole segment is the apodosis: 'I will send one tale to Rostam.'
segments.s162-g167: در آشتی = 'in/for reconciliation' (در + noun). با سیاووش = 'with Siyavash' (prepositional phrase marking the partner of reconciliation). نیز = 'also / likewise' attaches to the whole phrase, indicating this action is in addition to the previous one.
segments.s162-g168: بجویم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive 'I seek / I will seek' (classical use of subjunctive for intention). فرستم again is 1st-person singular 'I will send' (coordinated verb; Persian often strings verbs to express successive actions). بی‌اندازه چیز = 'innumerable / excessive things' where بی‌اندازه is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'boundless/excessive' modifying چیز ('things', here 'gifts' or 'matters').
segments.s162-g89: به + verbal noun زنهار دادن marks manner/instrument: 'by giving a warning' or 'with a warning.' زبان here is a noun meaning 'speech/utterance' and داد is the past tense verb 'gave'; شاه (the subject) appears at the end, a common Classical Persian order (verb before subject or verb at mid/end).
segments.s162-g90: کز introduces the subordinate clause ('so that/from which'), expressing purpose/result. نبیند is a negative present/subjunctive (optative) third-person form meaning 'may not see / would not see.' ازیشان is از + ایشان 'from them,' and گناه is the object 'sin/blame.' Word order places the clause-final verb after its object, typical in older Persian.
segments.s162-g91: زبان‌آوری is a nominal compound 'eloquence/skill of speech.' بود is the past copula 'was' linking the subject and predicate. بسیار is an intensifier 'very/much' modifying مغز, where مغز is used metaphorically as 'mind/wit.' The sequence Noun + بود + adjective phrase is a common copular construction.
segments.s162-g92: کجا is the interrogative 'where/whence.' برگشادی is an archaic/poetic past form (second-person singular past) of برگشاد 'opened/unfolded' — here used more generally as 'brought forth' or 'uttered.' سخنهای is the plural 'sayings/speeches,' and نغز is an adjective 'pithy/witty.' The verb appears after its object, as in classical word order.
segments.s162-g93: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus he said.' کز (classical form of که + از) introduces the content clause 'that/from which.' خواب is the noun 'sleep,' and شاه جهان is the compound 'king of the world' (شاه + جهان in genitive relationship). Note: گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said.'
segments.s162-g94: به marks direction/endpoint 'to' (به بیدرای آمد = 'came to wakefulness'). بیدرای (classical spelling of بیداری) is the noun/adjective 'awake/wakefulness,' آمد is the past 'came,' and سپاهی گران is an adjectival phrase 'a heavy/mighty army' (سپاهی = army, گران = heavy/mighty). The clause is roughly intransitive: 'a mighty host came to wakefulness.'
segments.s162-g95: یکی شاهزاده = 'a certain prince' (یکی as an indefinite 'one/some'). به + پیش اندرون indicates motion/toward the presence/inner court: به پیش 'to the presence' and اندرون 'inner quarters/inside.' Word order places the destination phrase after the noun, typical for narrative.
segments.s162-g96: جهان‌دیده is an adjectival epithet 'worldly/experienced.' با وی can mean 'with him' or 'for him' depending on context; here it functions as 'to/for him.' بسی rhenmon reads as 'very/great guide' (بسی intensifier + رهنمون 'guide'). The phrase places the descriptive epithets after the arrival, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s162-g137: یکی here is an indefinite determiner meaning 'a certain' or 'one' placed before the noun; انجمن is the direct object 'assembly/meeting'. ساخت is a past-tense verb 'made/formed' (3rd person singular). The preposition با 'with' attaches the following noun phrase (بخردان) as a companion/means.
segments.s162-g138: هشیوار and کارآزموده are adjectives placed before the head noun ردان; the coordinating و links the two adjectives. ردان is a plural noun here (those who 'drive off' or rout—i.e., commanders/men of action). The whole phrase is the noun phrase governed by the preceding با.
segments.s162-g139: بدیشان is a prepositional/pronominal phrase 'to them' indicating the addressees; چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying گفت. گفت is the reporting verb (past tense 'he said'); کز is the subordinating preposition/relative particle 'that/from which', introducing the content clause whose topic is روزگار.
segments.s162-g140: نبینم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive-form of 'to see' used here as 'I see (not)'; همی is an adverb adding continuity/emphasis 'now/at present/indeed'; بهره is 'share/portion'. جز is the negative/exclusive preposition 'except', and کارزار is the noun 'battle/struggle'—so the clause reads literally 'I see no share except battle.'
segments.s162-g141: بسا is a quantifier 'many' modifying the plural noun نامداران 'renowned men'. The relative particle که introduces a subordinate clause; بر دست من is a prepositional phrase literally 'upon/at my hand' used idiomatically to mean 'by my hand' or 'at my responsibility/power'.
segments.s162-g142: تبه شد is a compound meaning 'were ruined/was ruined' (تبه: ruin; شد: became/happened) and is passive in sense. به جنگ marks the occasion 'in/at the war', and اندرین is a demonstrative/adjectival 'in these/this' modifying انجمن 'assembly/meeting'—together 'were ruined in this battle/war of the assembly.'
segments.s162-g143: بسی is a quantifier 'many' (or 'much') modifying شارستان 'towns/cities'. گشت is the past-tense verb 'became/turned into' and بیمارستان is the predicate noun 'hospital'—thus 'many towns became hospitals.' Note the V follows the subject (typical Persian SOV order with slight freedom).
segments.s162-g144: This line repeats the same pattern: بسی 'many' + بوستان 'gardens' with نیز 'also' linking the clauses; شد is past 'became' and خارستان is the resulting state 'thorny thicket/wasteland'—'many gardens also became wastelands.'
segments.s162-g145: بسا is an emphatic quantifier/adverb meaning 'many' or 'many a' placed before the noun. باغ is the head noun 'garden'. کان is an archaic linking/relative particle here meaning 'that / as if' (introducing the following predicate). رزمگاه is the predicate noun 'battlefield'. منست is an archaic predicative form (contracted from 'من/ـِ است') functioning like a copula 'is' (here with a sense of 'is regarded as / amounts to').
segments.s162-g146: به هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'to/at every side' (به + determiner هر + سو 'side/direction'). نشان سپاه is a noun phrase 'the marks/signs of the army' (نشان = mark/sign; سپاه = army). منست again functions as the archaic copula 'are / are to be seen'. The word order is poetic: predicate after the noun-phrase, with منست tying the clause together.
segments.s162-g147: ز is the poetic causal preposition 'because of / from'. بیدادی is a genitive/attributive noun meaning 'injustice/tyranny' modifying the following noun. شهریار جهان is a possessive/genitive construction 'the ruler (شهریار) of the world (جهان)' — together 'the world's ruler' or 'the ruler of the world'.
segments.s162-g148: همه is the subject 'all' or 'everything'; نیکوی (an abstract noun/adjective) means 'goodness' or 'good things'. باشد here is a copular/auxiliary with the sense 'remains / is' (in older Persian it often carries a sense of continued state). اندر نهان is a locative phrase 'within / hidden' (اندر = in/within; نهان = hidden). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'all-good remains hidden inwards.'
segments.s162-g149: نزاید is from زاد/زای with an initial ن making it negative here ('is not born' / 'does not arise'); به هنگام is a temporal phrase 'at the time / in due season'; در دشت گور is a locative expression 'in the plain of the grave' (در = in; دشت = plain; گور = grave). Together: 'is not born in due season on the burial plain.'
segments.s162-g150: شود is the verb 'becomes / happens'. بچه باز is a compound noun: بچه = 'young/offspring' and باز = 'hawk/falcon', so 'the young hawk' (offspring of the hawk). را is the definite object marker/emphasis particle. دیده here is the noun 'eye' (not a participle), and کور is the adjective 'blind'. The clause literally orders as 'becomes the young-hawk (را) eye blind' → 'the young hawk's sight becomes blind.'
segments.s162-g151: نپرد is a present-form of پرید with an initial ن that gives a prohibitive/negative sense here ('does not / let not ... leap'). ز پستان is a locative/genitive 'from the breast/udder'. نخچیر شیر is a genitive phrase 'the prey/spoil of the lion' (نخچیر = prey/spoil; شیر = lion). Together: 'let not the spoil leap from the lion's breast.'
segments.s162-g152: شود is 'becomes'. آب is the subject 'water'. در چشمه خویش is a locative possessive phrase 'in its own spring/source' (در = in; چشمه = spring/source; خوی + ش = 'its own / its nature' where ش is the possessive suffix 'his/her/its'). قیر is the predicate noun 'tar / pitch'. The clause reads 'the water in its spring becomes tar.'
segments.s162-g185: The phrase lists two nouns joined by و (and): غلام و کنیزک (slaves and maidservants). The cluster به بر هم دویست is a poetic ordering meaning “altogether/taken together two hundred” — numeric modifier (دویست) follows the nouns, and به بر هم gives the sense “in total / together.”
segments.s162-g186: بگویش is an imperative form (بگو) with the enclitic -ش meaning “to him/her”: “tell him.” که introduces the content clause. با تو مرا جنگ نیست is an idiomatic older Persian way to say “I have no quarrel with you”: با تو = “with you,” مرا is the dative/possessive clitic (to me / for me) + را sometimes fused in poetry, and جنگ نیست = “there is no war/quarrel.”
segments.s162-g187: بپرسش is an imperative/subjunctive form (بپرس) with the enclitic -ش (“ask him/it” depending on context); فراوان is an adverb meaning “much/abundantly” and follows the verb here for emphasis. و او را بگوی is coordinated: او را (object/topic marker را with pronoun او) = “to him,” and بگوی is an imperative/infinitive form of گفتن used here as ‘tell’ (poetic forms of the verb are common).
segments.s162-g188: که introduces a content clause: ما سوی ایران نکردیم روی literally “we toward Iran did not do/turn face.” ما = we; سوی = toward; ایران = Iran; نکردیم is negative past (we did not do/act); روی here is ‘face/turn’ (i.e. ‘turn our face toward’). The whole idiom means “we did not go/turn toward Iran.”
segments.s162-g189: زمین تا لب رود جیحون: زمین = “land/country”; تا = “as far as / up to”; لب = “bank/brink”; رود جیحون = the Jayhoun (Amu Darya) River. مراست is a fused sequence (مرا + است) meaning “belongs to me / is mine” in older/poetic Persian, so the clause states the land up to the riverbank is mine.
segments.s162-g190: به سغدیم = “to/for Sogdiana” or “in Sogdiana” (سغدیم here names the region Sogdiana); و = and; این پادشاهی = “this kingdom/kingship”; جداست = the copula + adjective meaning “is separate/distinct.” The clause links territorial possession to Sogdiana and states that this rule is separate.
segments.s162-g191: همانست is همان + ست (copula) = “it is the same/that very (place).” کز is the archaic contraction of که از meaning “from which / from whom.” تور و سلم are proper names (Tur and Salm); دلیر is the adjective “brave,” placed after the names in this poetic word order.
segments.s162-g192: زبر شد is a verbal phrase meaning literally “became over/upward/turned over” (زبر can mean ‘above’ or ‘upside-down’ in older usage); جهان = the world; آن کجا بود زیر is a rhetorical sequence: آن = that, کجا بود = “where was (it)” (past interrogative), زیر = “below/under.” Together this makes a rhetorical image of the world being overturned and asking where its lower part then was.
segments.s162-g177: به زودی is an adverbial phrase meaning “quickly/soon.” بساز is an imperative (“make/compose”). سخن را uses the direct‑object marker را to mark سخن (“the phrase/line”) as the object of بساز. مه‌ایست is a nominal predicate (‘is the moon’ / ‘is moon‑like’) specifying what the composed line should be.
segments.s162-g178: ز here is the poetic preposition “from.” گزین کن is an emphatic two‑part imperative: گزین (choose) with the auxiliary/imperative کن (‘do’), producing ‘choose!’ لشگر is the source (‘from the army’), and سواری (rider/horseman) is the noun being selected; دویست (two hundred) supplies the number.
segments.s162-g179: به نزد (= به + نزد) marks motion/toward someone’s presence: ‘to the presence of.’ برخواسته is a perfective/participial form meaning ‘having gone up/approached’ or simply ‘came (before).’ Together they mean ‘came to Siyâvash’s presence.’
segments.s162-g180: This is a verbless/nominal clause describing what they brought: ز = ‘from/of,’ هر چیز = ‘every kind of thing,’ گنجی = ‘treasure’ (here as object), and بیاراسته should mean ‘arrayed/arranged/presented.’ The phrase therefore says they presented treasures of every sort. (The specific dictionary senses supplied do not include the ‘arrayed/presented’ meaning for بیاراسته, so that word is marked below accordingly.)
segments.s162-g181: از marks source (‘from’). اسپان تازی is a noun phrase ‘Arabian horses’ (اسپان = horses, تازی specifying Arabian/Arabian horse). به زرین links to the following noun as an adjectival phrase ‘with/of golden …’. The final word ستام here names the golden item or trappings associated with the horses (the precise poetic item is not represented among the provided senses and is therefore marked accordingly).
segments.s162-g182: Parallel structure to the previous half: ز = ‘from,’ شمشیر هندی = ‘Indian swords’ (شمشیر + adjective هندی), and به زرین نیام = ‘with golden scabbards’ (نیام = scabbard). The construction lists valuables taken from different types of equipment.
segments.s162-g183: یکی is the numeral ‘one/a certain,’ modifying تاج ‘crown.’ پرگوهر is a compound adjective ‘full of gems / jewel‑studded,’ and شاهوار is another adjective meaning ‘regal/lofty,’ so the three words form a compact noun phrase ‘one regal jewel‑studded crown.’
segments.s162-g184: ز here functions roughly as ‘of/amounting to’ in a valuation sense. گستردنی is a deverbal/adjectival form meaning ‘when spread/extended,’ صد = ‘a hundred,’ شتروار is a measure‑like adjective (‘per camel / camel‑like’ — used idiomatically for camel‑loads), and بار = ‘load/burden.’ The phrase quantifies the crown as equivalent to a hundred camel‑loads when spread out.
segments.s162-g65: بخوانیم is a first‑person plural hortative/imperative-like form meaning “let us call/summon” (here a gathered narrator’s injunction). بیدار‌دل is a compound adjective (بیدار “awake” + دل “heart”) qualifying موبدان (موبد, plural موبدان), so the whole phrase means “the mobeds who are wakeful‑hearted.” Word order is typical older Persian: verb (hortative) + adjective phrase + noun.
segments.s162-g66: از ... و از marks selection “from among … and from …”; the repetition emphasizes both groups. اخترشناسان here refers to star‑experts (astrologers), and بخردان is a plural noun meaning “the wise/prudent ones.” The construction indicates the mobeds are being chosen from those two circles.
segments.s162-g67: هر آنکس کزین دانش آگه بود is a relative clause: هر آنکس “whoever/any person” + کزین (archaic compound = 'of/from this') + دانش “knowledge” + آگه “aware/versed” + بود past copula “was.” Word order places the predicate adjective before the final copula, giving “whoever was aware of this knowledge.”
segments.s162-g68: پراگنده‌گر is an agentive noun (پراگنده +‌ گر) meaning “disperser/one who spreads or proclaims.” بر در شه is a locative phrase “at the king’s gate” (بر = on/at, در = gate/door, شه = king), and بود is the past copula. Together they form the predicate: “was a disseminator at the king’s gate.”
segments.s162-g69: شدند انجمن is a narrative past construction: شدند (they became/there formed) + انجمن (assembly), so “an assembly was formed” or “they gathered.” بر در شهریار is the locative phrase “before/at the king’s gate” (بر = at/toward, در = gate, شهریار = king).
segments.s162-g70: بدان تا introduces purpose: بدان (به + دان, here roughly “to know/thereupon”) + تا “so that/in order that.” چرا “why” introduces the content question; کردشان is an enclitic construction where کرد is ‘did’ and شان is the third‑person plural/object clitic, referring back to ‘them’/‘the matter’ (here taken as “had done”/“had acted”); خواستار functions as ‘the petitioner’/‘the one who sought’ (a noun). The clause therefore means “in order to know why the petitioner had acted thus.” Note some ambiguity in classical phrasing between object clitic readings, but the sense is purposive inquiry.
segments.s162-g71: بخواند here is a third‑person narrative form meaning “he called/summoned.” و is the conjunction “and.” سزاوار is an adjective “fitting/appropriate,” and بنشاند (archaic past form) means “he set/placed/caused to sit.” پیش means “before/in the presence of.” The sequence describes: he summoned them and properly seated them before him.
segments.s162-g72: سخن راند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: سخن “speech” + راند (past of راندن) meaning “he uttered/delivered” (i.e., he spoke). با هر یک از introduces the distributional phrase “with each one of,” and کم و بیش literally “less and more” is an adverbial formula meaning “more or less” or “to a lesser or greater degree.” So the whole means he addressed each person, some briefly, some at greater length.
segments.s162-g169: سران is the plural subject 'the leaders'. یک به یک is an adverbial idiom 'one by one' placed between subject and verb. پاسخ آراستند is a past-tense verbal phrase: آراستند (3rd person plural past of آراستن) here takes پاسخ as its object and means 'composed/gave a reply' (classical collocation 'پاسخ آراستن' = 'to give an answer').
segments.s162-g170: همی is a literary particle often signalling immediacy or emphasis before a verb (here functioning like 'then/indeed'). خوبی و راستی are coordinated direct objects 'goodness and uprightness/truth'. خواستند is 3rd person plural past of خواستن and here means 'they asked/desired'.
segments.s162-g171: که opens an explanatory / content clause ('for/that'). تو شهریاری is a verbless predicate construction common in Persian: 'you [are] king/sovereign' (copula omitted). و ما چون رهی links with 'and we, like wayfarers' where چون = 'like' and رهی = 'wayfarer'.
segments.s162-g172: بران is a contracted literary form (بر آن / بران) meaning 'upon that' or 'upon you' here linking to 'دل نهاده' (past participle 'placed/laid [their] heart'). نهاده is the past participle of نهادن 'to place', so دل نهاده = 'having placed their heart'. که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'; دهی is the present/subjunctive/imperative form of دادن 'you give' (2nd person), used here as a hortatory/optative 'that you give/issue' (i.e. 'so that you may issue commands').
segments.s162-g173: همه بازگشتند is a simple past clause 'all returned' (بازگشتند = 3rd person plural past). سر پر ز داد is a verbless descriptive phrase: سر = 'head', پر = 'full (of)', ز = poetic 'from/of', داد = 'justice' — together 'with heads full of justice'.
segments.s162-g174: نیامد is a negated past 'did not come' used impersonally here ('did not occur'). کسی را uses را to mark the affected person/experiencer 'to anyone'. غم و رنج یاد is a noun phrase 'the memory/remembrance of grief and suffering' (یاد = 'memory'), so the whole clause means 'the memory of sorrow and hardship did not come to anyone' (i.e. no one remembered grief).
segments.s162-g175: به گرسیوز is a prepositional phrase 'to Garsivaz' (به = 'to'). آنگه 'then' and چنین 'thus' are adverbial; گفت شاه is an inverted order common in classical Persian meaning 'the king said' (چنین گفت شاه = 'thus said the king').
segments.s162-g176: که introduces the content of the king's words ('that' / marking reported speech). ببسیج is a present/imperative-form verb with the intensive/prefixal ب- used in classical Persian meaning 'muster/gather' (here an instruction). کار functions as the object 'affair/forces' in this context (classical usage can mean 'troops/affairs to be mustered'). و links the two commands. بپیمای is another present/imperative-form verb from پیمودن 'to traverse/measure', here an order 'scout/measure (the) road'. راه = 'road'.
segments.s162-g193: از here introduces the cause/result clause ('because of' / 'with the event of'); ایرج is the proper name (the victim). که is the relative/conjunction introducing the subordinate clause. بر + بیگنه marks the agent in a passive construction (literally 'upon/by a stranger'), and کشته شد is the past passive ('was killed') with شد as the auxiliary.
segments.s162-g194: ز here is the poetic 'from / because of' marking source/result; مغز بزرگان = 'the minds/brains of the great ones' (genitive relationship expressed by noun juxtaposition). خرد گشته شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'wisdom was made to be gone / wisdom was undone' — گشته is a participle 'become/turned' and شد functions as auxiliary to produce the past/result meaning.
segments.s162-g195: ز توران به ایران places the two lands in a directional/relational phrase ('from Turan to Iran'); جدایی نبود is the predicate 'there was no separation/parting' with نبود as the past negative of 'to be'. Word order puts the relationship before the predicate.
segments.s162-g196: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'); باکین و جنگ form a coordinated nominal phrase ('vengeance and war') governing آشنایی (familiarity/experience). نبود again negates the clause ('there was no acquaintance/familiarity with vengeance and war').
segments.s162-g197: ز یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from God' (source/authority). بران گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner / of that sort' modifying the following predicate. دارم امید is the concrete idiom 'I have hope' (دارم = 'I have', followed by the noun امید).
segments.s162-g198: که introduces the content of the hope ('that...'). آید is a present/subjunctive sense 'may come / will come' expressing expectation. درود و خرام و نوید are coordinated nouns ('greeting, a proud/triumphant stride, and good tidings') forming the hoped-for items.
segments.s162-g199: برانگیخت is the past tense verb 'roused/raised (up)'. از شهر ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from the city of Iran' (source). ترا is the object 'you' expressed by the enclitic ت plus the object marker را (t + rā), attached after the noun phrase, so the verb precedes its object here.
segments.s162-g200: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause ('because / for that'). بر مهر is a prepositional phrase 'with affection / in love' (بر used with abstract noun مهر). دید is the past 'he saw'. از دلیران ترا places ترا ('you' with enclitic را) after a partitive/locative از phrase 'from among the brave', so the clause means 'he saw you beloved among the brave'.
segments.s162-g73: چنین گفت is a fronted adverbial + verb: 'thus said' or 'said thus.' با نامور موبدان is a prepositional phrase ('to/with the renowned mobeds') serving as the indirect object/recipient of the speaking; نامور modifies موبدان (plural). The verb گفت is a past narrative form placed after the adverbial.
segments.s162-g74: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/exclamatory opening (classical 'که ای ...' = 'O ...'); پاک‌دل and نیک‌پی are stacked adjectives modifying the following noun. بخردان is a plural vocative ('wise ones' / 'wise men') addressed by the speaker.
segments.s162-g75: گر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). این خواب و گفتار من is a coordinated noun phrase ('this dream and my speech/words'); من is the possessor of گفتار. در جهان ('in the world') locates the action; the word order places the condition's setting before the verb.
segments.s162-g76: ز کس بشنوم is the core of the conditional: ز (poetic 'from') + کس ('anyone') + بشنوم (1st‑person present/subjunctive/optative of شنیدن, here 'I should hear' / 'I hear') — a hypothetical hearing. آشکار و نهان are adverbial pairs ('openly and secretly') describing how the speech might be heard.
segments.s162-g77: یکی را is a marked object ('one person' with the object marker را). نمانم is first‑person negative/optative of ماندن used here as 'I will not leave' (i.e. 'I will leave none'). سر و تن به هم ('head and body together') is the object/complement expressing what will not remain; به هم means 'together.'
segments.s162-g78: اگر begins a subordinate conditional 'if.' زین here is a poetic/genitive-like 'از این' ('of/from this'); سخن is 'speech/utterance.' بر لب آرند دم is an idiomatic sequence: بر لب آوردن/آرند ('bring to the lip') = 'put on the lips / speak,' with آرند = third‑person plural present subjunctive/optative. دم ('breath/moment') is an expressive noun completing the idiom (i.e. 'if they breathe these words').
segments.s162-g79: ببخشیدشان is a verb with the third‑person object suffix -شان ('them'); بخشیدن can mean 'to grant/forgive/give' and here functions as a bestowal: 'give/grant them.' بیکران زر و سیم is the direct object ('boundless gold and silver') following that verb; زر and سیم are coordinated nouns.
segments.s162-g80: بدان is the fused prepositional/adverbial (به + دان/اینگونه) meaning roughly 'for that' or 'by that means.' تا introduces purpose/result ('so that'). نباشد is a negative subjunctive ('there be not' / 'may there not be'), followed by کسی ('anyone'). زو is poetic contraction of از او ('of/from it/him'), and ببیم here functions as a modal/optative ('we/one may see/learn of'); the whole phrase gives the purpose: 'so that no one may know of it.'
segments.s162-g209: تو شاهی is a nominal predication: تو (you) + شاهی (kingship/being king) with the copula omitted — a common poetic way to say “you are king.” و (and) links to the next clause. با شاه ایران بگوی uses با (with/to) + شاه ایران (the Shah of Iran) as the addressee, and بگوی is an archaic/poetic present-subjunctive/imperative form of ‘say/speak’ (tell/speak to); the verb comes after the prepositional phrase, which is normal in older Persian word order.
segments.s162-g210: مگر here functions as 'unless' (introducing the exception). نرم گردَد is a verbal phrase: نرم (soft/gentle) + گردد (third-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن = 'become'), so 'becomes soft' or 'is softened.' سر جنگجوی is a genitival phrase 'the head (spirit/courage) of the warrior'; classical poetry often omits the ezafe so the noun follows directly. The verb-and-adjective phrase is fronted, producing the order 'نرم گردد سر جنگجوی' (verb/adjective before its logical subject) typical of older poetic syntax.
segments.s162-g211: سخنها is the plural object 'words / speeches / talks' (سخن + -ها). همی is an archaic habitual/continuous particle meaning 'keep on / still / do continually' and combines with گوی (say/speak) to give a progressive/imperative sense 'keep speaking' or 'speak on.' با پیلتن is the prepositional phrase 'with Piltan' (address/companionship). Note that the object precedes the verb here, a frequent poetic/topic-prominent order.
segments.s162-g212: به چربی uses به + noun to form an adverbial phrase 'with fat / richly' (i.e., 'in a rich/embellished manner'). بسی here functions as an intensifier 'much / many' (archaic usage), modifying داستانها 'tales/stories' (داستان + -ها). بزن is the imperative of زدن ('strike / do / here: relate/tell'), placed at the end as the main verb: 'tell/relate many tales richly.'
segments.s162-g213: برین is the fused/poetic form of بر + این (literally 'upon/for this' but here functioning like 'take/bring this'); contextually it marks the demonstrative/object to be delivered. هم نشان = 'also/a token' (هم 'also' + نشان 'sign/token'). نزد رستم is the prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / to Rostam' (نزد used to indicate address/recipient). پیام ('message') appears as an appositive or second direct object — 'this token and message.'
segments.s162-g214: This segment lists additional items to accompany the message. پرستنده is a noun meaning 'worshipper/one who pays homage' or 'attendant' (here likely 'an envoy/attendant'); و is 'and'; اسپ is 'steed/horse'; و زرین ستام is 'and golden (زرین) ستام' with زرین adjectivally qualifying ستام. The series lacks an explicit verb in this segment because it is linked to the previous imperative 'take/bring.'
segments.s162-g215: به نزدیک او = 'to/near him' (به + نزدیک used adverbially with the pronoun او). هم‌چنین is 'likewise / in the same way' qualifying what follows. خواسته is the noun 'the requested thing / request' (from the participle of خواستن) and functions as the object of the imperative verb that follows.
segments.s162-g216: ببر is the imperative 'take/bring!' (second-person). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that.' شود is the third-person singular subjunctive/potential of شدن 'become / be (may be),' giving a purposive sense 'so that ... may be.' کار 'the affair' is the subject of شود, and پیراسته is an adjective 'adorned/arranged / put in order' describing کار. Together: 'Bring it so that the affair may be settled.'
segments.s162-g129: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause here = 'when' or 'if'. چشم زمانه is a possessive notion 'the world's eye/gaze' (زمانه = fortune/the world). بدوزم is the first-person present/subjunctive of دوختن used figuratively: 'to fix/fasten (one's) gaze on' (poetic image). به + گنج indicates the target ('upon/toward treasure').
segments.s162-g130: سزدگر is an archaic/poetic conditional particle meaning roughly 'if it befit' (literally 'fitting/if fitting'). سپهرم 'my heaven' = my fate or lot. نخواهد is the negative of خواستن in the 3rd person ('will not/will not desire'). به رنج = 'to/into suffering' (رنج = suffering). Together: 'if that befit, my fate will not desire suffering.'
segments.s162-g131: نخواهم is first‑person negative of خواستن ('I will not/wish not'); زمانه is 'fortune/the world' as the object ('I desire nothing of fortune'). جز آن کاو نوشت is an exception clause: جز آن = 'except that', کاو here is an archaic/relative use meaning 'which/that' (classical usage), and نوشت is past 'wrote/ordained' (referring to what God has inscribed/ordained).
segments.s162-g132: This is a clause of obligation: باید = 'must/ought'. The order is inverted for poetic effect (چنان زیست باید instead of باید چنین زیست): چنان 'thus/so', زیست here is the verb 'to live', and که یزدان سرشت is a relative/explanatory clause 'as God has fashioned/created' (سرشت is past 'created'). Together: 'one must live in the way God fashioned.'
segments.s162-g133: چو + past verb (بگذشت) = 'when/after'. نیمی ز = 'half of' (نیمی = half, ز = poetic 'of/from'). گردان سپهر is a genitive phrase 'the revolving heaven(s)' (گردان = revolving). The clause sets a time: 'when half the turning heavens had passed.'
segments.s162-g134: درخشنده خورشید is the agent phrase 'the shining sun'. بنمود is simple past of نمودن 'showed/made appear' and takes چهر 'face' as its object: 'the shining sun showed (its) face' = 'the sun appeared.' Word order (adj + noun + verb + object) is typical poetic word-placement.
segments.s162-g135: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/elders'. بدرگاه شاه is a locative/genitive phrase 'to the king's court/presence' (بدرگاه = at the court/into the presence of). آمدند is past plural 'they came'.
segments.s162-g136: This continues and qualifies the arrival: پرستنده is a participle/adjective 'worshipful' or 'reverent' modifying the coming men; و = 'and'; با کلاه آمدند = 'they came with hats' (با = with, کلاه = hats, آمدند = they came). The coordination links demeanor (پرستنده) with the manner (با کلاه).
segments.s162-g201: به بخت تو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/through your fortune' (به + noun + personal pronoun following the noun as possessor). آرام گیرد جهان places the adjective آرام before the verb گیرد and the subject جهان at the end; گیرد is a third‑person singular classical/current Persian form (here optative/subjunctive/energic) meaning 'may it become / may it be at rest'. Overall: 'By your fortune, may the world become calm.'
segments.s162-g202: شود is a third‑person singular verb meaning 'become' / 'may it be'. جنگ و ناخوبی is a coordinated noun phrase 'war and badness/ill‑will'; اندر نهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/within secret(ly)', with اندر (poetic 'in') preceding the locative adjective نهان. The verb precedes the subject phrase, a common flexible order in classical Persian.
segments.s162-g203: چو functions as the temporal conjunction 'when'. گرسیوز is the subject (proper name) and آید is the third‑person singular present/optative 'comes'. به نزدیک تو is a locative phrase 'near you' using به + نزدیک with the possessive pronoun تو after the locative noun (typical Persian order).
segments.s162-g204: به بار آید is an idiomatic verbal cluster meaning 'come to fruition / be realized' (به + بار + آمد/آید). آن رای تاریک تو is the object phrase 'that dark counsel of yours' with demonstrative آن before رای and the possessive pronoun تو following رای. آید again is the third‑person verb 'comes / will come to pass.'
segments.s162-g205: چنان چون is an emphatic conjunction 'just as' / 'in the same way that'. به گاه means 'at the time of' (به + گاه), and فریدون is the proper name; گرد here is a classical verbal/epithet element meaning roughly 'when Fereydun was in power / when he went about' (classical usage giving the sense 'in the days of Fereydun'). Word order is temporal phrase followed by the name and verbal epithet.
segments.s162-g206: که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that'. گیتی is the direct object 'the world'; ببخشش is a classical verbal form meaning 'granted it' (ببخش + enclitic ـش = 'it'), here 'gave/granted it'. به گردان is a prepositional phrase 'to the commanders/troops' (به + گردان), and سپرد is the past tense 'he entrusted/handed over'. The sequence shows a past narrative action 'he granted the world and entrusted it to the commanders.'
segments.s162-g207: ببخشیم is first‑person plural present/subjunctive or hortative 'let us forgive' / 'we should forgive' (ب + verb is a classical imperfective/optative pattern). و coordinates the clauses. آن رای بازآوریم means 'let us bring back / restore that counsel' with بازآوریم a first‑person plural present/subjunctive 'we restore / let us restore.'
segments.s162-g208: ز جنگ و ز کین is a repetitive poetic construction 'from war and from hatred/vengeance' (ز = from). پای بازآوریم is an idiom 'bring back the foot' = 'withdraw our step' / 'turn away'; بازآوریم is again first‑person plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us withdraw / bring back.' The whole segment expresses an exhortation to desist from war and enmity.
